nameMeanings.name: Delamare
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: Norman, French, Huguenot:  See  De la Mare  .

variants.name: De la Mare
meanings: Norman, French, Huguenot: locative name for someone from (French de ‘of, from’) any of the numerous places in France named La Mare (‘the pool’). Introduced by the Normans, the name was later augmented by Huguenot bearers fleeing religious persecution in France and the Low Countries in the 16th century. Compare Dallimore .

variants.name: Dallimore
meanings: Norman, English: (i) locative name denoting someone from (Old French de) any of the numerous places in France named La Mare (‘the pool’). Compare De la Mare and Delamare . (ii) locative name from Middle English mere ‘lake, pond’ or mor(e) ‘marsh, bog; moor’ (from Old English mere and mōr respectively), with common confusion of these words. Compare Moore .

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

en.lifeSummary: William Delamare was born in 1530, in Llangennith, Glamorgan, Wales, United Kingdom. He had at least 1 daughter with Alice Ferch Thomas Llyd. He died in 1580, in Glamorgan, Wales, United Kingdom, at the age of 50.
